revision.comment: Bot: fix merged QID Q11423842 → Q109046180 apply
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土管]]
[[File:Dacian Water Pipe.JPG|thumb|right|300px|{{ill|Dacia|en|Dacia|qid=Q173082|lt=Dacian|1=Dacia}} clay pipe.]] '''Clay pipe''' (土管, Dokan) refers to a cylindrical pipe made of baked {{ill|Clay|en|Clay|qid=Q42302|lt=clay|1=Clay}}.<ref name="ReferenceA">Kojien{{Full citation needed|date=March 2017|qq=}}</ref> Pipes commonly referred to as "clay pipes" but made of concrete are technically called "Hume pipes" and are distinct from true clay pipes. Hume pipes are primarily used as conduits in construction sites, whereas clay pipes are utilized for equipment that transports acidic or alkaline liquids or sewage. However, most of these applications have now transitioned to {{ill|PVC|ja|塩化ビニール|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/PVC|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ポリ塩化ビニル|1=Polyvinyl chloride|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q146368}} pipes, with clay pipes rarely used today. == Overview == [[File:土管（陶管）.jpg|thumb|250px|Various examples of clay pipes]] Clay pipes are used in {{ill|drainage channels|ja|排水路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/drainage channels|1=drainage ditch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1345747}}, {{ill|Chimney|en|Chimney|qid=Q170477|lt=chimneys|1=Chimney}}, and other installations.<ref name="ReferenceA"/> To connect pipes, they are designed with an expanded section on one end to serve as a {{ill|socket|ja|ソケット (配管)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/socket|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or joint. Some are left in an unglazed, bisque-fired state, while others are finished with a glossy glaze. Clay pipes are shaped, dried, and baked in a kiln. They come in various diameters, starting from about 6 cm. The colors range from reddish-brown to yellowish hues and dark brown. In Japan, clay pipes were extensively used for sewage systems starting in the Meiji era, particularly for medium-scale drainage systems until around the 1970s. Currently, unglazed clay pipes are used for {{ill|subsurface drainage|ja|暗渠排水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/subsurface drainage|1=culvert drainage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11514909}} systems in farmland, residential areas, and sports fields. As a type of {{ill|Pottery|en|Pottery|qid=Q11642|lt=ceramic|1=Pottery}}, they excel in durability, corrosion resistance, and chemical resistance, offering a long functional lifespan. == Origins and History == === China === The oldest clay pipe unearthed in China dates to around 2100 BCE and was discovered at the Pingliangtai Ancient City site in {{ill|Henan|en|Henan|qid=Q43684|lt=Henan Province|1=Henan}}.<ref>[https://www.easyatm.com.tw/wiki/%E5%B9%B3%E7%B3%A7%E5%8F%B0%E5%8F%A4%E5%9F%8E%E9%81%BA%E5%9D%80 "Pingliangtai Ancient City Ruins," Baike Knowledge], accessed April 29, 2020.</ref> These pipes, used for drainage within city walls, were approximately 30–45 cm long and featured rope-like patterns on their surfaces. During the {{ill|Qin (state)|en|Qin (state)|qid=Q34756|lt=Qin dynasty|1=Qin (state)}}, rare stone pipes were used alongside clay pipes. By the {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han dynasty|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 漢|1=Han dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7209}} (202 BCE–8 CE), brick-like "tile pipes" were common, and in the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang dynasty|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐|1=Tang dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9683}} (618–907 CE), tile pipes were widely used for buildings, while non-tile clay pipes are exceedingly rare.<ref>{{Sfn|Kakita|1992|pp=5-6|qq=}}</ref> === Japan === Chinese clay pipe techniques were introduced to Japan via the Korean Peninsula in the 6th century.<ref>{{Sfn|Kakita|1992|p=3|qq=}}</ref> Ancient Japanese clay pipes were made using two techniques: pottery-type and tile-type. Limited examples have been unearthed. The earliest discovery was in the outer west gate of {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|qid=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera|1=Asuka-dera}}, dating to the early 7th century. These pipes, made of tiles, were discovered during a 1996 excavation by the {{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|en|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qid=Q3297691|lt=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties}}. Measuring 20 cm in diameter and up to 56.5 cm in length, they represent the earliest clay pipes in Japan.<ref>{{Sfn|Onogi|2006|qq=}}</ref> Clay pipes were manufactured extensively during the Edo period in {{ill|Tokoname|en|Tokoname|qid=Q209683|lt=Tokoname|1=Tokoname}} using {{ill|multi-chamber climbing kilns|ja|連房式登窯|es|Horno escalonado multicámara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/multi-chamber climbing kilns|1=multi-chamber climbing kilns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11639897}}.<ref>[https://www.tokonameyaki.or.jp/about_tokonameyaki.html "History of Tokoname Ceramics"], Tokoname Ceramics Cooperative Association, accessed April 22, 2020.</ref> During the Meiji era, potter {{ill|Koiye Kataju|ja|鯉江方寿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koiye Kataju|1=Hōju Koie|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11674237}} improved manufacturing techniques to produce highly durable, leak-resistant clay pipes.<ref>{{Sfn|Kakita|1992|p=3|qq=}}</ref> === Europe === The earliest clay pipe, dated to around 3000 BCE, was found at the {{ill|Knossos|en|Knossos|qid=Q173527|lt=Knossos|1=Knossos}} Palace in Crete. These pipes featured interlocking flanges for secure connections.<ref>{{Sfn|Kakita|1992|p=4|qq=}}</ref> In 1858, mechanical forming technology for clay pipes was invented in Ottweiler, France, spreading rapidly across Europe and globally.<ref>{{Sfn|Kakita|1992|p=5|qq=}}</ref> == Concrete Alternatives and Challenges == [[File:土管8290491.jpg|thumbnail|260px|Concrete pipe abandoned by the seaside]] From the Showa period, concrete pipes became more common. They are produced by pouring concrete into molds and allowing it to set. Sizes vary, with some large enough for trucks to pass through. Concrete pipes are primarily used for {{ill|Sewerage|en|Sewerage|qid=Q156849|lt=sewage systems|1=Sewerage}}, but they face issues such as corrosion from sulfuric acid, which forms through microbial activity in sewage.<ref>[https://www.jswa.go.jp/g/g5/g5m/im/pdf/i7.pdf "Concrete Corrosion from Sulfuric Acid"], Japan Sewage Works Agency, accessed April 29, 2020.</ref> == Cultural Symbolism == Clay pipes, often depicted in manga such as {{ill|Doraemon|en|Doraemon|qid=Q235311|lt=Doraemon|1=Doraemon}} and {{ill|Osomatsu-kun|en|Osomatsu-kun|qid=Q2546336|lt=Osomatsu-kun|1=Osomatsu-kun}}, symbolize post-war peace and optimism in Japan.<ref>Natsume Fusanosuke, *The Power of Manga*, Shobunsha, 1999, pp. 48–49.</ref> Children frequently used construction sites as playgrounds, with empty lots featuring stacks of clay pipes serving as backdrops for community activities.<ref>[https://www.o-uccino.jp/article/posts/9137 "Why Were There Pipes in Empty Lots?"], Yomuno, accessed February 9, 2021.</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Vitrified_clay_pipe|en|Vitrified_clay_pipe|qid=Q7937239|lt=Vitrified clay pipe|1=Vitrified clay pipe}} * {{ill|Sewerage|en|Sewerage|qid=Q156849|lt=Sewage systems|1=Sewerage}} * {{ill|Hume pipe|ja|ヒューム管|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hume pipe|1=Hume pipe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11330269}} * {{ill|Gakuran|en|Gakuran|qid=Q6592316|lt=Gakuran|1=Gakuran}} – "Dokan" (土管) pants feature a cylindrical silhouette reminiscent of clay pipes. == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite journal|author=Kakita Tomizo|title=Changes in "Clay Pipe" Usage from Antiquity to the Meiji Era|date=1992|journal=Tokoname Folklore Research Bulletin|volume=5|pages=3–29|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210625002159/http://tokoname-tounomori.jp/download/index.html|qq=}} * {{Cite journal|author=Onogi Ruriko|title=Analysis of Clay Pipes Found at Asuka Temple's Western Gate|date=2006-01-31|journal=Shisen|volume=103|pages=32–51|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10112/11716|qq=}} * {{Architecture-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|土管|version=104038744|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:土管]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q109046180}} [[Category:Building materials]] [[Category:Facilities]] [[Category:Plumbing]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Machinery]]
